ranked_opportunities.scqa: The SAR Buyer Side
ranked_opportunities.verdict: EnduroSat is a Bulgarian software-flexible satellite manufacturer at a US-build inflection: $147M raised in six months (Founders Fund, Riot, Google Ventures), new EnduroSat USA CEO Rusty Thomas (former DARPA Project Blackjack) tripling Denver from 5 to 15+. The March 2026 MetaSensing X-band SAR partnership (PhoeniX, StarSAR-X on FRAME, ~0.125m resolution) is the most commercially interesting move and a direct match to Paul's KSAT decade selling NRT SAR services into oil and gas, maritime, and environmental compliance. Tier 2, pursue with caveats; the early-conversation question is whether this seat is commercial-services-led or pulled toward the DC gov-pipeline build (Shield Space, SDA-supplier outreach).
ranked_opportunities.doc_note: Full deep dive completed 2026-05-15. Top SCQA: The SAR Buyer Side (91.5/100). Recommended resume: EO/Maritime (primary), Ecosystem/Platform (alternate). Open commercial-vs-gov scoping question with Rusty Thomas before applying.

ranked_opportunities.hook: MetaSensing X-band SAR-as-a-service offering on FRAME platform is a direct match to Paul's KSAT SAR services decade. First BDM in a 5-to-15 Denver build under an ex-DARPA CEO with $147M US capital pressure.

blockers.detail: JD states 'primarily on-site and in person at the office' in Denver, CO; Paul is in Taos, NM. Soft blocker, Denver is the easiest US aerospace city for Paul to relocate to (short flight from ABQ, Mountain West).

ranked_opportunities.verdict: Orbit Fab is a post-PMF space infrastructure company scaling its commercial, civil, and government customer base. The role emphasizes formal proposal/capture management and government contracting BD motions, areas where Paul has adjacent but not direct experience. His 15 years in commercial space (KSAT satellite services, GFW data partnerships) demonstrate deep sector knowledge and relationship-building strength, but his work was market-creation and partnership-driven, not GovCon proposal/capture. The JD's requirement for "Proven experience leading proposal development and capture management processes" and "Minimum 5 years of business development experience" creates a soft gap: Paul's titles and documented work do not emphasize formal BD or proposal management, capping RF at 5 and IL at 5. The role is growth-stage execution (SA 6), not greenfield new-build, and the company's situation is adjacent to Paul's patterns (regulated buyers, multi-stakeholder complexity) but not a textbook match (SPM 7). Compensation ($130-165k + equity at a Series B space infrastructure company) and hybrid Colorado location are both reasonable (CO 6, GC 5). Weighted total: ~54 (Tier 3). This is a sector-aligned role at a mission-driven company, but the BD/proposal emphasis and Paul's lack of formal GovCon capture experience make it a stretch. Pursue with caveats: Paul would need to frame his KSAT/GFW work as de facto BD and address the proposal/capture gap directly.

blockers.detail: JD requires 'Minimum 5 years of business development experience' and 'Minimum 8 years in a professional environment.' Paul has 15 years of commercial experience but his title/role history (KSAT, GFW) was not explicitly 'business development' titled roles, creating a potential paper-match gap in how his experience maps to the BD title requirement.

JD states 'Previous experience working within the aerospace or space industry' as a required qualification. Paul has 10 years at KSAT (space ground services, satellite data) and 5 years at GFW (satellite data buyer), totaling 15 years in commercial space sector across infrastructure, data, and services, this is a strong match, not a gap.
Role is hybrid in Lafayette, CO office. Paul is currently in Taos, NM and open to relocation.

ranked_opportunities.source_note: Pinned by Paul 2026-06-09: high personal interest, elevated by choice above Rainmaker. Raw fit score 56.0; Tier-3 reflects a formal capture/proposal-management gap, not geography.

ranked_opportunities.role: Senior Director, Subscription & Data Solutions @ LevelTen Energy | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role requires deep domain experience in the renewable energy PPA market that Paul does not possess. The JD explicitly requires "10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem" and "Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors." Paul's 15 years of commercial infrastructure experience is in satellite monitoring (oil & gas, fishing surveillance, maritime), not renewable energy transactions. While the role structure, commercial-product bridge, subscription packaging, data monetization strategy, maps beautifully to Paul's skills from GFW (building commercial licensing for an open platform, multi-stakeholder deal architecture), the sector mismatch is a hard blocker. RF capped at 5 because the responsibilities fit Paul's profile but the required domain does not. IL capped at 4 because Paul cannot credibly claim the named renewable energy market experience or customer base. The role is well-compensated and the stage is interesting (post-PMF data platform scaling subscriptions), but Paul would need to explain a full sector pivot from space/maritime to renewable energy, and the JD signals they want someone who has "been living and breathing this market for years."

blockers.type: Hard requirement: 10+ years renewable energy market experience
blockers.detail: JD requires '10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem.' Paul has 15 years in commercial space/satellite sector (KSAT 2011-2021, GFW 2021-2026) but not in renewable energy PPAs, energy procurement, or the utility/developer ecosystem that LevelTen serves. His infrastructure work was water/wastewater utility security (PA Consulting) and satellite monitoring, not renewable energy transactions.

blockers.type: Hard requirement: Significant commercial/sales experience selling to energy buyers, developers, utilities, advisors
blockers.detail: JD requires 'Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors and understand how deals get done.' Paul's sales experience is in satellite monitoring services to oil & gas, fishing surveillance, and maritime clients. He has not sold to renewable energy buyers, PPAs, or clean energy developers.

ADM is also an ag conglomerate rather than a mission-driven climate venture, which is outside Paul's stated target profile. Pass unless Paul wants to use this as a carbon-market learning conversation.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a carbon credit sales role at an Asia-focused carbon project platform. While the mission aligns strongly with Paul's climate/sustainability focus and the entrepreneurial framing is appealing, the core work is enterprise carbon credit sales ,  managing pipeline, closing deals, forecasting ,  not building a commercial function or creating demand in a new market. The JD explicitly requires "7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management" and describes the role as "responsible for the entire carbon credits sales cycle." Paul's KSAT and GFW experience demonstrates commercial development and multi-stakeholder deal architecture, but he is not a quota-carrying enterprise seller. RF capped at 5 because the role shape does not match Paul's market-builder identity. IL at 5 because Paul has relevant carbon/climate sector knowledge (GFW commercial licensing, KSAT EO for environmental compliance) but lacks the named "carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." SA scores 6 because Rubicon is described as expanding and the role is "newly added due to business expansion," but the role itself is about selling an existing product (carbon credits) to enterprises, not building the commercial function. SPM scores 7 because multi-stakeholder carbon credit deals and navigating complex corporate buyers fits Paul's pattern, even though the role itself is misaligned. Singapore preference is a soft geographic blocker.

blockers.detail: Role has 'strong preference for candidates based in Singapore.' Paul is based in Taos, NM. While location is described as 'flexible,' the preference signal creates friction.
Role is 'entire carbon credits sales cycle' including 'managing pipeline, executing account strategies' ,  this is quota-carrying enterprise sales, not market-building.

SPM scores 6 for multi-stakeholder coordination fit, but the three patterns don't directly apply to internal platform PM work. This is a "right domain, wrong chair" scenario.

ranked_opportunities.role: INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT AND SALES MANAGER VACANCY
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a solid commercial development role in Earth observation with genuine market-building elements (APAC, LATAM, Middle East, Africa expansion), which aligns well with Paul's EO sector experience and pattern of opening new markets. However, the role appears to be primarily managing established product lines in new geographies rather than building a commercial function from scratch, the company already has technologies and institutional relationships, and the hire is tasked with 'accelerating' and 'expanding' rather than creating. The Bilbao-based location with no stated remote option is a significant practical consideration requiring international relocation to Spain. RF scores 6 because the role fits Paul's commercial builder profile and EO sector background, but the JD emphasizes 'strategic sales, account management' experience over pure market-creation, and there's no evidence this is a first commercial hire. SA scores 5 because the role involves genuine new-build work in new geographies, but it's expansion of an established capability, not standing up a function from zero. IL scores 5 because Paul's 15 years in EO and government/institutional sales are strong, but the JD's emphasis on 'aerospace, space, Earth observation' sector experience as what will 'help you stand out' (not required) suggests they may prefer candidates with deeper space-hardware or institutional-space backgrounds than Paul's commercial-monitoring focus. SPM scores 7 because selling Earth observation to governments and institutions in emerging markets involves multi-stakeholder coordination and some regulatory shaping, which is adjacent to Paul's patterns. GC scores 3 due to the Bilbao location requirement with no remote flexibility stated.

blockers.detail: Role is based in Bilbao, Spain. Paul is currently in Taos, NM and would need to relocate internationally. This requires consideration of visa/work authorization for Spain and significant relocation.

watch_list.category: Passive Microwave Sensing / Soil Moisture EO
watch_list.why: Dutch. Global satellite-based soil moisture and vegetation data. Agriculture, insurance, commodities buyers. Fits Paul's data licensing and commercial vertical-building experience.
watch_list.roast: VanderSat has been flying under the radar, pun intended, doing passive microwave sensing for soil moisture and vegetation while everyone else chases optical and SAR. The company has real customers in agriculture, insurance, and commodities, which are the three verticals where satellite data companies go to find out if their technology is a product or a science project. Soil moisture is one of those variables that matters enormously to people who know it matters and is invisible to everyone else. Paul built the GFW commercial licensing program by identifying where a free platform needed structured commercial relationships, VanderSat has the opposite problem, they have a commercial product and need to convince buyers that passive microwave data is worth paying for when optical imagery is everywhere and free adjacent. The company has been around long enough to survive the trough, which means they have revenue and a business model that works, but they are in the growth phase where they need someone who can articulate why soil moisture data is not just nice to have but a genuine competitive advantage for the buyer. Paul has done this twice, once convincing oil majors to pay KSAT for monitoring they did not think they needed, once convincing insurance companies to pay GFW for vessel data when AIS was supposed to be free. VanderSat is Dutch, which means the company is probably under capitalized by Silicon Valley standards but over engineered by everyone else's, and the culture will be direct in a way that Paul will find refreshing after too many ATS systems that reject him with inspirational messages. The risk is that VanderSat gets acquired by a larger geospatial player and the commercial role evaporates. The opportunity is that they are at the stage where the next fifty customers look like the first fifty and Paul has built that playbook twice.

watch_list.why: Madrid-based, $130M Series B (April 2026), AI-optimized satellite constellation for real-time geospatial data, L3Harris manufacturing partnership. Paul's EO data licensing and multi-stakeholder deal architecture experience is direct fit.
watch_list.roast: Spanish startup that just closed one-thirty million to build AI-native satellites and you are somehow supposed to believe they cracked the unit economics problem that murdered half the EO sector in the last eighteen months. They paired with L3Harris to build the sensors which means someone in Madrid convinced a Florida defense contractor that their architecture is worth betting ruggedized hardware on. The pitch is on-board inference to cut latency and the customer list sounds like every other pitch deck you saw at CommEO last year but the money is real and the timing is sharp. You spent a decade at KSAT explaining to oil majors why they should pay for something they did not know they needed and then you did it again at GFW selling licenses on top of a free platform. Xoople is selling intelligence not imagery and they are doing it in a market where half the buyers still think Planet is expensive. If they survive the next funding winter they will need someone who knows how to build commercial arguments where the technology is two steps ahead of the customer's brain. You have done this twice. They have one-thirty million and a hardware partner and no idea how to sell to the enterprise buyers who are still figuring out what derived data products even are. Also they are in Madrid which is not Tromso but it is not San Francisco either and you already proved you can work in Europe without losing your mind.

watch_list.why: Zurich-based, $11.8M Series A, building Earth Intelligence Platform using satellite data and AI to simulate atmospheric physics. Climate risk modeling customer base overlaps Paul's insurance vertical work (AXA Climate, British Marine/QBE).
watch_list.roast: Swiss startup that raised eleven million euros to build an AI model that learns physics from satellite data because apparently numerical weather prediction is not complicated enough already. They integrate optical sensors, radiometers, radars, and GNSS radio occultation which sounds like they are ingesting every feed on the market and hoping the model figures out what to do with it. The climate tech angle is real and the insurance vertical is live which means someone at AXA or Swiss Re is taking their calls but the question is whether they are selling forecasts or selling the idea of better forecasts. You closed AXA Climate and British Marine QBE deals at GFW by finding the moment when the status quo felt riskier than the pilot and that exact skill is what Jua needs if they are going to convince parametric insurance underwriters to trust a black box model over fifty years of actuarial tables. They are based in Zurich which is the world capital of people who do not take risks they cannot quantify and you are the person who taught conservative buyers how to buy things they did not know existed. The team is technical and the funding is adequate and the market timing is good if you believe extreme weather is going to keep breaking underwriting models. The hard part is not the science, it is making the commercial argument stick when the buyer's entire career depends on not being wrong. You have done that in offshore energy, in fisheries surveillance, in maritime risk. Do it again but this time the customer already knows they have a problem.

watch_list.category: Synthetic Aperture Radar / Middle East EO
watch_list.why: Saudi Arabia-based, $2.6M seed (Tonomus/Aramco VC), building SAR constellation for MENA region (deforestation, border security, disasters, oil leakage). Sovereign EO play with regional focus, fits Paul's government/commercial deal-building and oil/gas monitoring background.
watch_list.roast: Saudi-backed SAR startup that closed two point six million from Tonomus and Aramco Ventures to build the ArabiaEye constellation because apparently the Middle East decided it wants its own satellites now and SARsatX is the chosen vehicle. The pitch is SAR plus optical fusion for the MENA region with use cases that read like a KSAT proposal from 2014 when you were flying to Perth and Houston explaining why oil majors should pay for proactive monitoring. The sovereignty angle is real and the funding sources tell you exactly who the customer is going to be but the question is whether they are building a commercial business or a subsidy recycling program for Vision 2030. You spent years in the North Sea and offshore Australia getting ENI and Woodside to move NOPSEMA toward requiring remote sensing in environmental plans and that playbook works if the regulatory pressure is genuine. Saudi has the budget and the strategic intent but you would be selling into a market where the government is the customer, the investor, and the regulator at the same time which is either the easiest deal structure you ever touched or the most politically suffocating. They are Seraphim alumni which means someone in London thought the business model was credible and the MENA market is underserved and growing fast but you already passed on UP42 because you did not want to explain Saudi ownership to North American enterprise customers. This is the inverse problem but the question is the same which is whether you want to spend the next three years navigating Riyadh instead of Taos.

watch_list.why: Vertical specialist, own constellation, analytics-first model, mentioned alongside ICEYE/Planet in 2026 EO industry roundup
watch_list.roast: Calgary builds hyperspectral satellites promising to see what nobody else can see, which is basically the EO industry in a single sentence. You spent a decade at KSAT selling the idea that someone should pay for what free Sentinel data almost delivers, so you understand exactly what Wyvern is up against. They raised money, they launched hardware, and now comes the hard part where a corporate sustainability officer has to explain to her CFO why 100 spectral bands matter when three bands are free. The technical capability is real. The customer education timeline is long. You have done this movie before, including the part where the pilot works, the customer loves the insights, and procurement says we will think about it and circle back next quarter. But hyperspectral is not vaporware anymore, and if someone is going to make the commercial case stick it will be a team that understands the gap between spectrum and spreadsheet. You closed Shell on a 2.5 year cycle selling oil spill monitoring. Wyvern is selling molecules you cannot see to buyers who are not sure they need them yet. Same game, higher resolution, narrower margin for error. Check the careers page and decide if you want to build the business case one pharma supply chain at a time or wait until someone else proves the market exists.

watch_list.why: US-based, 15cm resolution, VLEO at 250km, defense/intel buyer base, proprietary propulsion for drag mitigation
watch_list.roast: San Francisco decided that flying satellites at half the altitude of everyone else would deliver twice the resolution, which is the kind of engineering logic that works until you hit the atmosphere at 17,000 mph and your orbit decays in three months. EOI Space patented a propulsion system to solve the drag problem, raised the money, and is now in the phase where they have to prove the tech works and someone will pay for 15cm imagery when 30cm is already available and mostly good enough. You have sold Earth observation data to oil majors, governments, and NGOs who all asked the same question at some point, which is why this costs so much. The answer at KSAT was always because physics, and the answer at EOI is the same except the physics are harder and the customer is either DoD or a three-letter agency with a black budget. They are hiring across the board, which means they are scaling to launch, and VLEO is not a fantasy anymore even if the unit economics are still speculative. The company has AFRL partnerships, which is the Space Force putting chips on the table, and that matters more than customer slide decks. This is a builder role at a hard-tech company solving actual propulsion and thermal problems, not a SaaS play with a satellite wrapper. If you want to sell to the commercial market you will spend three years explaining why sub-meter matters. If you want to sell to the government you will spend three years getting clearance and learning RFP compliance. But the imagery will be the best anyone outside of NRO has ever flown commercially, and that counts for something when the entire industry is converging toward higher resolution and lower altitude. The question is whether you want to build the market or wait until someone else proves it.

watch_list.why: EU-based, farm-scale MRV for soil carbon, addressing the measurement gap in ag carbon markets, 4.5M hectares monitored
watch_list.roast: Copenhagen is trying to convince European farmers that soil carbon is worth tracking and commodity buyers that the resulting credits are worth buying, which is approximately as hard as it sounds. Agreena combines remote sensing with soil sampling and machine learning to quantify carbon sequestration in a carbon pool that is invisible, highly variable, and moves around depending on rainfall and what the farmer did last Tuesday. You built a commercial licensing function on top of a free platform at GFW, so you know exactly what it feels like to sell something people think should be free or are not sure they need yet. Agreena has 77 million in funding and 4.5 million hectares under monitoring, which means they survived the 2025 voluntary carbon market stagnation that killed Nori and a dozen other soil carbon startups. They are one of the survivors, which in this market means they either have product-market fit or very patient capital or both. The regulatory tailwind is real, EUDR and EU Methane Regulation are creating demand for verified ag data, and the insurance and food company channels are starting to move. But soil carbon MRV is still a translation problem. You have to sell the science to the farmer, the financial case to the off-taker, and the methodology to the auditor, and all three conversations happen in different languages. This is the kind of complex stakeholder sale you did at KSAT when you were moving NOPSEMA toward requiring proactive monitoring, except the regulator has not mandated it yet and the pilot customers are still figuring out if the data is decision-useful. If you like the idea of building a carbon market from scratch in a category where the measurement is hard and the incentives are misaligned, this is that. If you want a mature sales process with clear buyer intent and a short cycle, this is not it. But the sector is not going away, and Agreena is one of the few players with enough traction and capital to make it past the pilot phase.

watch_list.why: AI-driven subsurface resource discovery, $115M Series C April 2026, six-plant buildout plan, satellite/remote sensing for conventional geothermal identification
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT watching operators drill dry holes in the North Sea because the seismic looked pretty but the reservoir was a lie. Zanskar trained AI on subsurface datasets and satellite observations to find conventional geothermal nobody else saw, then raised $115 million in April to build six plants in the Western US by 2029. It is the same pattern you lived in oil and gas surveillance, finding the signal in noise nobody bothered to process right, except this time the reservoir is heat instead of hydrocarbons and the customer is a hyperscaler buying baseload instead of a super major buying monitoring. They are at the build-things-in-the-ground stage now, which means commercial risk is real and the org will need people who know how to talk to utilities, offtakers, and regulators without the AI pitchbook. You know what happens when the technology works but the commercial infrastructure has not been built yet because you were the one building it for KSAT from 2011 to 2016. Zanskar is pre-revenue but post-doubt, which is the hardest window to hire into because everyone wants proven traction or ground-floor equity and nobody wants the messy middle where you are selling something that exists but has not been sold at scale yet. That is the only window you have ever wanted to work in. If they have a partnerships or commercial role open you should take the call, because the subsurface competence is legitimate and the capital is real and the offtake market exists, which means the only question left is who builds the deals. Check their site.

watch_list.why: Farmer-led reforestation credits with satellite AI MRV, forward carbon contracts, app-based verification, smallholder-focused at scale
watch_list.roast: The carbon credit world has spent five years trying to figure out how to make smallholder agroforestry credits credible without sending an auditor to every ten-hectare plot in Kenya twice a year at a cost that eats the entire margin. Earthbanc built an MRV stack using satellite remote sensing, AI modeling, and a mobile app that farmers actually use to document planting and survival rates, then sells the credits as forward contracts so the finance flows before the trees are twenty years old. It is a harder commercial problem than selling imagery to oil majors because the buyer has to trust a methodology that blends field data with orbital inference, and the seller has to trust that the smallholder in Malawi will keep the trees alive when the incentive payment is two years away. You know that tension because you lived it at GFW, building a commercial licensing program on top of an open-access platform where the fundamental question was always whether the buyer believed the data was worth paying for when a free version existed. Earthbanc has the same structural problem, which is that premium MRV has to justify itself against cheaper practice-based estimates, and the only way to win that argument is to show up with a customer who paid for it and a methodology that held up under scrutiny. They are early, probably ten people, definitely venture-backed, and absolutely in the part of the market where you understand the commercial tension better than most of the people already working there. If they have a partnerships or market development role it is worth a look, because the MRV problem is real and the buyer pool is growing and someone has to build the sales engine that turns the technology into revenue. Check their site.

watch_list.why: Digital MRV for smallholder rice methane reduction using IoT sensors, drones, satellites, blockchain records. India-focused, Fraunhofer partnership, ACRAT project deploying now.
watch_list.roast: Flooded rice paddies produce 25 times more methane per hectare than anyone wants to talk about and CarbonMint built a digital MRV system combining IoT soil sensors, drone spectral imaging, satellite coverage every five days at ten-meter resolution, and blockchain transaction records to prove that farmers in India adopted alternate wetting and drying practices that cut water use and methane emissions without killing yields. It is the kind of system that sounds impossible to operate at scale in fragmented smallholder landscapes until you see that they clustered farmers into cooperatives so the mechanization and digital tools are affordable and the monitoring is distributed across plot boundaries that used to be invisible to any satellite coarser than three meters. The commercial model treats carbon credits as icing, not cake, which means the revenue has to come from input cost savings and yield improvement or the whole thing collapses when carbon prices weaken. You spent two years at GFW trying to convince buyers that the derived product was worth paying for when the base layer was free, and you learned that the only buyers who pay are the ones who need a structured relationship and verified outputs, not just access to the data. CarbonMint has the same dynamic playing out in agricultural carbon, except the verification burden is heavier and the buyer pool is narrower and the regulatory framework is still being written. They are working with Fraunhofer University and deploying in the field right now, which means they are past proof of concept and into the phase where someone has to go build the commercial partnerships with corporates, insurers, and carbon registries. That is where you would add value, because the MRV is credible and the agronomic intervention is proven and the only question left is who will pay for it at scale and how to structure those deals. Check if they have a role open in market development or partnerships, because the problem is real and the solution is being deployed and the commercial function is the missing piece.

watch_list.category: EO / Carbon MRV / AI Foundation Models
watch_list.why: Seed-funded, University of Waterloo origin, building multimodal foundation models trained on satellite remote sensing for carbon removal and nature conservation. Direct analog to Paul's GFW platform-building experience. Backed by HF0, Inovia, Propeller, AWS partnership.
watch_list.roast: Coastal Carbon is building AI foundation models for the natural world, which sounds like the kind of thing a PhD team pitches when they realize memorizing Reddit is not actually useful. They are right. Seed-funded out of Waterloo, backed by people who sold actual companies, working with customers in carbon removal and conservation and trying to make remote sensing data legible to people who are not remote sensing PhDs. The hard part is not the model. The hard part is never the model. The hard part is finding the buyers who will pay before the model is perfect, and then staying close enough to those buyers that you build what they actually need instead of what won a conference poster session. Paul built the commercial licensing program at GFW by first convincing the organization that selling was consistent with the mission. Coastal Carbon will have a version of that fight, because every climate-data startup does. The question is whether they can close the first three deals before the seed round runs out. If they can, they are the kind of company that turns satellite data into revenue instead of into another grant application. If they cannot, they will be extremely well cited.

watch_list.why: Trellis 2026 climate adaptation category finalist. Satellite and sensor-based environmental monitoring, climate risk platforms. Buyers are enterprise and government. Fits Paul's risk-vertical framing (AXA Climate, British Marine) and his comfort selling derived-data products into decision-making workflows.
watch_list.roast: Helix Earth showed up on the Trellis 2026 climate adaptation shortlist, which means they are either solving a real problem or they are very good at filling out applications. Climate adaptation pulled five and a half billion dollars in 2025, up sixty four percent, because everyone finally admitted that we are not preventing the weather and someone needs to sell the forecast. Helix Earth is in that game. Early warning systems, extreme weather forecasting, the tools that help cities and companies figure out whether to sandbag or evacuate. The commercial argument is easier than carbon markets because the buyer does not have to believe in your methodology, they just have to believe it might rain. Paul spent two years convincing Shell that they could not afford to not know where the oil was. Helix Earth has a simpler version of that pitch. The question is whether they have built a product that enterprise risk teams will actually deploy or whether they have built a very beautiful demo that wins awards and dies in procurement. The companies that survive in this space are the ones that close the boring contract with the boring insurance company in month six, not the ones that win the best-in-show at the climate tech summit. If Helix Earth has revenue, Paul should call them. If they do not, he should wait six months and then call them.

watch_list.why: AI platform for natural disaster prep and climate risk reporting. Insurance and enterprise buyers. Growing climate adaptation segment up 64% to $5.5B.
watch_list.roast: Beehive wants to automate your climate risk reporting so you can finally explain to the CFO why the factory flooded again without building a deck from scratch. It is disaster response software written by people who have clearly read the insurance RFPs and understand that nobody actually wants to do scenario modeling by hand. The product is an AI platform that helps companies prepare for wildfires, floods, and the other Greatest Hits of the Anthropocene, which is useful until you remember that half the companies buying this software are preparing for risks they are actively creating. You spent a decade at GFW explaining to people that satellite data tells you what already happened and helps you figure out what to do next. Beehive is selling the same insight to the Fortune 500 except instead of illegal fishing it is their own supply chain on fire. The climate adaptation market is the fastest growing segment in climate tech right now, which tells you everything about how well mitigation is going. The company is early stage, venture backed, classic YC-style build-fast-sell-faster energy. If you can tolerate the irony of selling resilience software to the companies least interested in preventing the problem, this is a decent opener into the insurance and risk analytics verticals where people actually pay for data products instead of just talking about them. You know how to position satellite derived insights. This is that, but the satellite is pointed at the client.

watch_list.why: Mobile lidar and AI for forest measurement. Replaces manual tree surveys. Carbon credit MRV and forestry management buyers.
watch_list.roast: Gaia AI replaces the guy with the calipers and the tape measure, which is a low bar but also the entire business model for half of climate tech. They built a mobile lidar rig that captures 3D point clouds of individual trees while you walk through the forest, then the AI measures trunk diameter, height, and all the other numbers that foresters pretend to care about until it is time to actually cruise a stand. The output is a precise inventory instead of statistical guesswork extrapolated from a few sample plots, which matters a lot if you are trying to sell carbon credits or prove to an investor that the forest you just bought is worth what you said it was. This is MIT bred, venture backed, classic deeptech playbook. You know the routine. The product works, the use case is real, but the commercial question is always whether the customer will pay enough to justify the cost of hardware and the pain of adoption. Forestry moves slow. You spent years at KSAT convincing oil majors that remote sensing was cheaper than waiting for a spill to hit the news. Gaia is selling the same pattern to a different sector, except the buyers have smaller budgets and longer decision cycles. The carbon MRV angle is the wedge. Verification is the only part of the voluntary carbon market where people will actually pay for precision, because the alternative is getting called out by journalists with access to the same satellite data you are pretending to sell. If you can stomach another sector where the revenue model depends on convincing risk averse institutions to adopt new technology before the regulation forces them to, this is that fight. You have had this conversation before. You know how it ends.

watch_list.why: Electrochemical CO2 reformer for steel blast furnace waste gas. Converts emissions to syngas fuel. Steel and heavy industry decarbonization.
watch_list.roast: Helix Carbon is trying to fix steel, which is responsible for seven percent of global emissions and exactly zero percent of the sectors that venture capitalists understand. They built an electrochemical reformer that takes the CO2 coming out of a blast furnace and turns it into syngas that the steel mill can burn as fuel, which is a closed loop that sounds great until you remember that the steel industry has been resisting meaningful change since before your grandparents were born. This is MIT spinout, deep hardware, long development cycles, the kind of company where the first commercial deployment is five years out and the addressable market is every steel mill on earth but the actual market is whoever is desperate enough or subsidized enough to try it first. You know this pattern. You sold to oil majors. Steel is worse. The technology is legitimate. The problem is real. But the path from working prototype to revenue at scale requires navigating procurement bureaucracies, multi year pilot agreements, and plant managers who have been doing it the same way since 1987 and see no reason to stop now. The carbon recycling value prop is compelling in theory, which is exactly the kind of thing that gets you a meetings with sustainability VPs and no follow through from operations. If you want to spend the next three years explaining electrochemistry to people who do not care about electrochemistry but do care about whether this thing will shut down the line, this is the role. You have done harder. Whether you want to do it again in a sector with even less urgency than offshore oil is the real question.

watch_list.why: UK-based, won EUDR compliance contract with ICE, combines LiDAR/SAR with AI for forest carbon verification. Partnership with Upstream Tech. Direct EO-to-carbon-market bridge, exactly Paul's GFW-Planet intersection.
watch_list.roast: Space Intelligence started because two guys got tired of measuring trees by hand in Africa and decided satellites were easier. Fifteen years later they have an EUDR contract with the EU and a partnership with Upstream Tech, which means somebody in Brussels finally figured out that you cannot verify deforestation compliance with a clipboard and a intern. Their tech uses LiDAR, SAR, and custom software to turn public satellite data into something carbon buyers will actually trust, which is the entire game. They are small, UK-based, and selling into the exact regulatory-pressure-creates-demand cycle Paul spent a decade building at KSAT. The founders are academics who figured out how to commercialize, the customer base is split between project developers and regulators, and the product is verification-as-a-service in a market where nobody trusts anything. Paul has spent twenty years on both sides of the satellite data licensing table and built the GFW commercial function on top of an open platform. Space Intelligence is selling the same argument to a different vertical. The difference is that forest carbon has actual money behind it now and EUDR has a compliance deadline. They need somebody who knows how to move a regulator, structure a three-party deal, and explain why high-latency non-commercial MRV does not compete with real-time intelligence. That is the whole Planet Labs negotiation in a different wrapper. If they are hiring for partnerships or commercial, Paul should be on the phone yesterday.

watch_list.why: California-based, built Lens platform for landscape monitoring and MRV. Partnership with Space Intelligence. Geospatial data aggregation and delivery model mirrors GFW's vessel tracking but for land use and carbon. Clear BD/partnerships opportunity.
watch_list.roast: Upstream Tech built Lens, which is a geospatial data platform that makes satellite imagery and land change data actually usable for people who are not GIS nerds. They just partnered with Space Intelligence to put forest MRV data on the platform, which means they figured out that selling access is easier than selling insights. The model is SaaS for climate data, the customer is everyone from NGOs to carbon project developers, and the value proposition is that you do not need a PhD to see what is happening on the ground. Paul built the GFW commercial licensing program by figuring out where derived data has private sector value and then constructing the commercial arguments to get buyers there. Upstream is doing the same thing but for land instead of ocean. They have venture backing, a California base, and a product that lives in the gap between raw satellite feeds and bespoke consulting. The challenge is the same one Paul faced at GFW, which is that a lot of the underlying data is free or cheap and the margin lives in packaging, credibility, and the relationship. They need somebody who has sold complex data products into risk-averse buyers, built partnerships with data providers who might also be competitors, and can translate technical capability into a use case that a procurement officer will actually sign. That is the ASN subsea cable deal and the Planet three-party negotiation in the same conversation. If Upstream is building out partnerships or enterprise sales, Paul is the obvious call.

watch_list.why: India-based but global reach, digital MRV platform for supply chain traceability and carbon verification. Selling into EUDR, ESG reporting, and carbon markets. Combines satellite data with ground truth. Partnerships-heavy model.
watch_list.roast: TraceX is building digital MRV infrastructure for supply chains, which means they are trying to solve the problem of proving that your coffee or your timber or your beef did not come from a place that just got turned into a parking lot. They use satellite data, geospatial intelligence, and blockchain to create audit trails that regulators and buyers will accept, and they are selling into EUDR compliance and ESG reporting mandates, which is regulatory-driven demand at scale. The company is based in India but works globally, the customer base is enterprises who need to prove things, and the model is platform-as-a-service with a partnerships-and-integrations layer on top. Paul spent three years at GFW building commercial licensing on an open data platform and a decade at KSAT figuring out how to sell satellite monitoring into regulatory pressure points. TraceX is the same pattern but in a different sector. The challenge is not the tech, it is figuring out which buyers will pay before the regulation actually lands and building the partnerships with data providers, standards bodies, and implementation partners to make the whole thing work. That is exactly what Paul did with ENI and NOPSEMA in Australia, except this time it is supply chain compliance instead of offshore oil. If they are building out BD or partnerships and need somebody who understands multi-stakeholder deal architecture and how to use pilots to set precedent, Paul wrote that playbook. The only question is whether they want somebody who will tell them the truth about how long these sales cycles actually take.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based soil carbon MRV at 90 percent cost reduction, profitable while competitors burn cash, 2M tonnes annually, smallholder focus in Global South. Direct Planet/GFW analogy but for agriculture carbon markets.
watch_list.roast: So you built a satellite-based MRV platform for smallholder farmers in India and became profitable while every SF-based carbon startup with a Sequoia term sheet lit money on fire and folded. Earthshot Prize, TIME 100, $35M in project financing, and you are processing dirt in nine countries using the same remote sensing stack Paul spent a decade selling to people who did not want to buy it. The whole voluntary carbon market is a dumpster fire in 2026, but somehow Boomitra is removing two million tonnes a year and making money doing it, which makes you either the only honest operator in the space or the best liar. Paul has spent his entire career explaining to risk-averse enterprise buyers why satellite data they cannot see with their own eyes is worth paying for. He closed a Planet deal at 95 percent off list by finding the one buyer whose use case was structurally non-competitive. Boomitra is doing the same thing but for farmers who have been shut out of carbon finance entirely. If you can convince him that soil carbon MRV is not just practice-based handwaving dressed up in Landsat pixels, and that someone will actually pay for the credits in 2027, he will build you a commercial licensing function from scratch. If you cannot, he will tell you in the first meeting and save everyone six months.

watch_list.why: Diversified carbon removal (regen ag, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced weathering), selling through Puro/Isometric/Verra/Gold Standard, just raised $20M Series B, India-based with global ambitions. Commercial licensing and data partnerships fits Paul's exact profile.
watch_list.roast: You are hedging every carbon removal pathway simultaneously, which in 2026 makes you either very smart or very confused. Regenerative agriculture, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced rock weathering, and you sell verified credits through five different registries because you cannot decide which methodology will survive the next wave of investigative journalism. Raised $20M leading into a Series B round in February 2026, which means someone believes the diversification play, or at least believes that one of your four bets will hit and the rest can be written off quietly. Paul has done this exact dance. At GFW he built a commercial licensing program on top of a free platform by identifying the narrow sliver of use cases where someone had to pay for a structured relationship even when the data was technically available for nothing. He knows how to parse real differentiation from noise. Varaha is either building the only carbon platform that will matter when half the current MRV stack goes bust, or you are spreading so thin that you will do nothing well. The question Paul will ask in the first meeting is this: which one of your four pathways has actual signed offtake at a price that covers your MRV cost and still makes the farmer money? If the answer involves the words pipeline or projected, the meeting is over. If you have a spreadsheet and a bank wire, he will listen.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based, combines satellite monitoring with field sensors and mobile apps for continuous MRV, integrates socioeconomic and biodiversity metrics alongside carbon. Multi-stakeholder data complexity aligns with Paul's ecosystem deal experience.
watch_list.roast: Amsterdam-based agroforestry MRV using satellites, field sensors, and mobile apps for continuous verification, plus you track socioeconomic and biodiversity co-benefits because apparently carbon alone was not hard enough to sell. This is either the future of credible nature-based solutions or the most expensive way to prove that a tree is still a tree. You are trying to do what everyone in the voluntary carbon market says they want, which is holistic measurement that goes beyond tonnage, but nobody has figured out how to price holistic in a market that barely prices carbon. Paul spent three years at KSAT building an oil spill monitoring service that required satellite operators, weather model providers, government regulators, and oil majors to all agree on what constituted actionable intelligence. Then he did it again at GFW with a three-party Planet deal where the third party originally thought GFW was going to destroy their business. Treecycle is a multi-stakeholder MRV problem disguised as a technology problem. The sensor network is the easy part. The hard part is convincing a corporate buyer that the biodiversity and community impact numbers you are reporting are worth paying extra for when they can buy cheaper credits from someone who only measures carbon and calls it a day. If Paul joins, he will not build you a sales pitch. He will build you a licensing framework that separates the buyers who actually need your full stack from the ones who are just checking a box. And he will tell you in month two if that market actually exists.

watch_list.why: Bremen-based VHR optical constellation, first launch Q4 2026, €5.3M seed, ESA Copernicus contract, dual-use (climate + defense)
watch_list.roast: Bremen, Germany. Five million euros seed. First satellite Q4 2026. Very high resolution optical, up to hourly revisit, climate and defense dual-use. You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to people who did not want to buy it, then another three years at GFW trying to convince an open-access nonprofit that commercial revenue was not a betrayal. Marble is pre-revenue, pre-launch, and dual-use, which means they are selling a capability nobody has seen yet to buyers in two different regulatory universes. The good news is you have done this before. The KSAT pitch in 2011 was a satellite service that did not exist yet sold into a market that did not believe it needed one. You won Shell and Exxon anyway. Marble has an ESA Copernicus Contributing Missions contract, which is the European equivalent of someone important enough to matter saying yes before anyone else did. They need someone who can walk into a room with a German defense procurement officer and a Norwegian environmental regulator on the same day and make the same satellite sound like the answer to completely different questions. That is the whole job. The risk is they are heavily EU-funded in a geopolitical moment when EU defense budgets are finally moving but commercial EO margins are not, and if they cannot cross the valley between those two revenue streams in the next eighteen months the runway ends. You would be building the commercial function in parallel with the first satellite going up, which is either perfect timing or twelve months too early depending on whether the imagery is any good. No one knows yet. The founders came out of the German satellite industry, not from a sales background, so they have built the thing and now need someone to figure out who pays for it and why. If you can figure that out before the money runs out, this is the kind of role where you get to define the category. If you cannot, it is a very expensive science project. Either way, it is exactly the kind of too-early market-creation problem you have spent twenty years not walking away from.

watch_list.category: Thermal Imaging EO / Urban Climate Intelligence
watch_list.why: India-based thermal IR constellation, $5.5M raised, urban heat island pilots (Ahmedabad, Tumakuru), climate resilience + agriculture applications
watch_list.roast: Bangalore, India. Five and a half million raised. Thermal infrared constellation. Urban heat islands, air pollution monitoring, climate resilience at scale. They have pilots running in Ahmedabad and Tumakuru covering four hundred thousand people, and they have forty-two million in letters of intent, which means someone is interested enough to sign something but not interested enough to wire money yet. You know that gap. You lived in it for two years at KSAT waiting for Shell to move from maybe to yes. The thermal piece is interesting because it is not another visible-light me-too play, and the climate application is directly in your lane, but this is an India-based startup selling into Indian government and enterprise buyers, which is a market you have never worked and a procurement environment that makes NOPSEMA look transparent. The opportunity is that thermal intelligence for urban climate adaptation is a real and growing need, and if they can prove the model in India the export case writes itself, but the risk is that you would be trying to build a global commercial function for a company whose early traction is entirely domestic and whose go-to-market motion you have no pattern-matching for. They need someone who understands how to sell satellite-derived climate data to cities, which you have done, and who can build the case for why thermal is worth paying for when optical is mostly free, which is a harder sell. The honest question is whether you want to learn a new market from scratch or whether you want to go somewhere your KSAT and GFW networks and regulatory pattern-matching actually apply from day one. If the answer is the former, and you are comfortable with the India market risk and the fact that their revenue is still mostly Letters of Intent, this is a genuine climate mission with differentiated technology. If the answer is the latter, there are plays closer to home.

watch_list.why: Boston-based, $175M raised, DeepSky AI-native weather satellite constellation, insurance and enterprise weather intelligence platform
watch_list.roast: Tomorrow.io just raised another $35 million to launch its DeepSky satellite constellation and expand AI-driven weather intelligence, which means they are still burning venture capital to out-forecast NOAA while convincing enterprise customers that proprietary weather models are worth the subscription. They have been at this for years. The pitch is always the same. Better forecasts, faster updates, hyperlocal precision for logistics, energy, aviation, insurance. The buyers are real. Delta, Uber, JetBlue. The use case is obvious. Weather drives billions in operational cost and nobody trusts the free forecast when money is on the table. You know this market. You sold monitoring services to industries that could not afford to guess wrong. The difference is that you were selling evidence, not predictions. ICEYE sells what happened. Tomorrow.io sells what might happen. The edge case for you is partnerships and data licensing. They are launching satellites, which means they will need to explain to customers why they should pay for Tomorrow.io radar plus Tomorrow.io satellite plus Tomorrow.io models instead of stitching free sources together. That explanation is a commercial argument, not a features list. You have made that argument in three different industries. If they have a partnerships role open and you can stomach selling probabilistic intelligence instead of deterministic proof, the pitch writes itself. The risk is that weather is a features war and every model is an API call away from commodification. You would be walking into that fight on purpose.

watch_list.category: Very Low Earth Orbit Optical EO
watch_list.why: 10cm resolution from 200-300km VLEO orbit, first launch 2026, highest-res commercial optical imagery on the planet
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT watching customers pay Maxar and DigitalGlobe eye-watering rates for sub-meter imagery because the use case demanded it and no one else could deliver. Albedo is betting they can undercut that entire value chain by flying satellites so low they burn through fuel like a Tromsø winter burns through diesel, achieving ten-centimeter resolution with commercially available optics instead of billion-dollar government glass. First satellite launches this year, and if the orbit holds and the optics deliver, they will own the defense and critical infrastructure surveillance market overnight. The technical risk is huge. The regulatory risk is bigger. But you negotiated Shell and ExxonMobil into proactive monitoring contracts when the entire industry said it was impossible, so you know exactly what happens when a new capability crosses the threshold from science project to something a procurement officer can no longer afford to ignore. Mountain View, sixty million raised, and a pitch that sounds insane until you remember that Planet flew cubesats when everyone said imagery required school bus sized satellites. You have spent twenty years on both sides of the EO licensing table. You know which conversations matter. This one matters.

watch_list.why: Vertically integrated platform delivering turnkey climate data products (GHG monitoring, methane detection, wildfire risk), not raw imagery
watch_list.roast: Most EO companies are still trying to sell raw imagery to customers who have no idea what to do with a ten-terabyte TIFF file and a vague promise that insights exist somewhere inside it. Muon Space looked at that model and said no thanks, we will build the entire stack, fly the sensors ourselves, run the processing, and deliver the actual product the customer needs, which is a number they can put in a spreadsheet or a polygon they can show their board. Methane leak detection, wildfire risk scores, greenhouse gas monitoring. The pitch is that they are selling intelligence, not data, which is the only way this market ever scales beyond a few hundred technical buyers. You built GFW commercial licensing by identifying where derived products had private sector value even when the underlying data was free. Muon is doing the same thing but backwards, keeping the data closed and productizing the outputs. They have raised serious money, they are flying real hardware, and their customer list includes exactly the types of organizations that have budget authority and no patience for data engineering projects. The challenge is the same one you hit at GFW, which is that the pipeline narrows fast once you move past the early adopters. But if anyone can take a climate data product into insurance, agriculture, and financial risk modeling, it is someone who already did it once with fishing vessels and knows where the commercial conversation lives.

watch_list.why: AI-based hyperspectral imaging for floods, wildfires, ecosystem detection. Singapore-based, EO domain fit.
watch_list.roast: Singapore hyperspectral startup doing flood and wildfire detection from orbit. You spent a decade at KSAT watching every polar-orbiting EO satellite of relevance fly overhead while you built oil spill monitoring into a multi-vertical portfolio, so you know exactly what hyperspectral can and cannot do. RIFFAI is selling AI-driven ecosystem shift detection to a market that still thinks Landsat 8 is cutting edge. The technology is real, the operational question is whether their buyers understand the difference between spectral signature and actual ground truth, because you learned that lesson in 2013 when ExxonMobil told you poor performance in many areas. The pitch is climate adaptation intelligence, the business model is enterprise SaaS to governments and insurance, and the org is young enough that they probably do not have a commercial function yet. You would be the one building it. Check the careers page, ask who their first five customers were, and if they say UN or AWS partnership without naming a paying contract, you know the distance between demo and revenue.

watch_list.why: Accelerated mineral weathering with patented MRV sensors. Ocean-based CDR, fits maritime and carbon background.
watch_list.roast: Enhanced weathering startup out of the US doing ocean alkalinity measurement with patented CDR and MRV sensors in real time. They are combining mineral weathering, carbon chemistry monitoring, and integration with existing coastal infrastructure, which sounds like someone read the same enhanced weathering papers you did and decided to productize the sensor layer instead of the removal itself. You negotiated a 1.2 billion hectare Planet deal and built an ASN subsea cable partnership from a cold call, so you understand the three-party deal structure this requires. The coastal infrastructure play is smart because it avoids the we need to build everything from scratch trap that kills ocean CDR companies. The MRV sensor is the differentiated asset. The commercial question is whether they are selling carbon credits, selling MRV as a service to other removal projects, or licensing the sensor tech, because those are three different businesses. If the answer is all three, they have not decided yet, which means you would be the one deciding. That is either the perfect builder role or a signal that they have technology without a business model. Poke around, see if they have offtake, and if the CEO has a PhD and no commercial experience, you have seen this movie before.

watch_list.why: Carbon credit integrity ratings. SaaS platform for buyers, investors, traders. Fits GFW commercial licensing and market-making pattern.
watch_list.roast: Carbon credit ratings platform telling buyers which offsets are real and which are marketing. You built a commercial licensing program on top of a free platform at GFW by identifying where the open-access product creates demand for a verified commercial relationship, so you know exactly what Calyx is doing. They are the S&P for voluntary carbon markets, except the market is a dumpster fire and everyone knows it, which is why a ratings business exists at all. The SaaS model is sound, the customer base is buyers and brokers who got burned once and need someone else to do diligence, and the timing is decent because CORSIA Phase 1 is starting to force real money into the market. The question is whether corporate buyers trust a third-party rater or whether they just stop buying credits entirely, which is the actual trend. You would be selling credibility as a product, which is either the highest-margin business in climate tech or the hardest, depending on whether the voluntary carbon market survives the next eighteen months. If they have fifty enterprise customers and recurring revenue, take the call. If they are still venture-funded with no clear path to breakeven, it is a bet on market structure that might not materialize. You know how to evaluate that, you did it with GFW insurance deals under a Moore Foundation pilot when no one knew if the channel would exist.

watch_list.why: Carbon Direct just acquired them (Nov 2025). AI + satellite forest carbon MRV at scale, Verra DMRV pilot partner, direct GFW analog on the commercial licensing paradox.
watch_list.roast: Pachama built the forest carbon MRV stack everyone said was impossible, then got bought by Carbon Direct before they could really figure out whether being a platform or a marketplace was the actual business. You spent a decade at GFW navigating the open data paradox, Paul. Pachama had the opposite problem: they had the tech, the Verra pilot, the satellite + AI credibility, and buyers who actually wanted to pay, but they still ended up inside someone else's go to market engine. That should tell you something. The MRV layer is critical infrastructure but infrastructure rarely wins as a standalone play unless you own distribution, and Pachama didn't. What they did own: four years of training data nobody else has, relationships with every serious forest carbon project developer in the voluntary market, and the only DMRV framework Verra actually trusts. Carbon Direct gets the platform, but they also inherited a team that knows how to talk to both sides of a three party negotiation. You closed Planet at 95 percent off because you understood competitive harm wasn't about price, it was about latency and use case separation. Pachama's version of that insight is that MRV isn't a product, it is the trust layer that makes a market. If you can articulate why continuous satellite monitoring replaces episodic site visits without saying the words "transparency" or "integrity" like a consultant, you should be talking to whoever runs partnerships there now. They need someone who has actually sold data derived from free platforms into commercial contexts and lived.

watch_list.why: No-code satellite data platform purpose-built for NbS project integrity and digital MRV. Paul's buyer profile: non-technical project developers who need EO analysis without hiring a data scientist.
watch_list.roast: Earth Blox is what happens when someone finally admits that most carbon project developers cannot code and do not want to learn Python just to run a Sentinel 2 NDVI time series. They built a no code EO analytics platform aimed directly at the MRV trust problem in nature based solutions, which sounds like product market fit until you remember that the voluntary carbon market spent 2024 and 2025 in an existential crisis and most buyers still do not know whether to pay for practice based credits or satellite verified ones. You have spent fifteen years explaining why EO data is worth paying for to people who were not sure they needed it yet, Paul. Earth Blox has the same problem but in a market with even less commercial maturity. What they do have: former PwC UK Nature Analytics lead as co founder, actual traction with NbS project developers, and positioning at the exact moment compliance markets like Singapore and Brazil are demanding digital MRV that is both rigorous and scalable. If you can walk into a room and explain how a no code EO tool saves a project developer six months and forty thousand dollars on third party verification without over promising what satellites can actually measure at field scale, you might be the first person they have talked to who actually gets it. The company is UK based but the market they are building is global and the sales motion is ecosystem driven, not transactional. You built GFW's commercial function by identifying where derived data products have value that the underlying open data does not. Earth Blox's version of that is: the analysis is the product, not the imagery. If you can sell that story without making it sound like SaaS vaporware, you should.

watch_list.why: Satellite MRV for soil carbon and regen ag. Major player in ag carbon space, $3.2B market growing 12.8% CAGR. Buyer ecosystem includes Planet, Corteva, food companies.
watch_list.roast: Regrow Ag is in the soil carbon MRV business, which means they are selling the promise that satellites and models can tell you how much carbon is in dirt you cannot see, on farms you will never visit, to buyers who are one news cycle away from deciding the whole thing is a scam. The ag carbon market is theoretically enormous and actually a minefield. You know this, Paul. You spent two years at KSAT convincing oil majors that synthetic aperture radar could find their spills before Greenpeace did, which required both technical credibility and the commercial argument that reputational risk is quantifiable. Regrow's version of that pitch is: regenerative agriculture sequesters carbon, we can measure it at scale with satellite imagery and modeling, and food companies will pay for verified credits to meet scope three targets. All of that is true until someone asks how much ground truthing you actually did and whether practice based estimates are just expensive guessing. What Regrow has going for it: Planet Labs and Corteva money, an MRV platform that integrates optical and radar, and positioning in a three billion dollar market that is growing fast because compliance is coming whether the tech is ready or not. The CBAM and EPA methane mandates are real regulatory forcing functions. If you can sell a complex multi provider EO ecosystem to a conservative enterprise buyer and make the ROI case before the pilot ends, Regrow needs that. They have the tech, they have the funding, but the commercial path from agronomic SaaS to carbon credit infrastructure is not obvious and nobody there has built a licensing program on top of a platform that was designed to give the data away for free. You have.

watch_list.why: Extreme weather forecasting for insurance, utilities, emergency response using satellite and radar
watch_list.roast: Climavision is building radar and satellite weather forecasting for the people who will actually pay when a forecast matters. Not consumer weather apps with animated raindrops. Not free NOAA wrappers with venture cosmetics. Insurance carriers pricing parametric risk. Utilities trying not to burn California again. Emergency managers who need thirty minutes, not thirty hours. You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to Shell when they realized not knowing was more expensive than knowing. Same structural move here. The buyer already has weather data. What they do not have is the system that tells them which forecast to trust when the models diverge and the claim is seven figures. Climavision sells decision confidence, not data feeds. The company is early enough that a partnerships role would mean building the commercial function around a technical capability that works but has not yet found all its revenue channels. You have done that twice. Once in Tromso when KSAT had satellites and no customers. Once at GFW when you had to convince the board that commercial licensing was not mission drift. If you can explain to an actuary why radar beats satellite at convective nowcasting and to a satellite operator why their L-band data completes the radar picture, you are the translator they need. The risk is that everyone thinks they can build a weather company until they meet their first enterprise buyer and realize weather is infrastructure, not features. You know the difference. That is the edge.

watch_list.category: Climate Intelligence / Satellite Data for Agriculture
watch_list.why: Israel-based,sat data + climate models for land value chains, fits EO and climate mission overlap
watch_list.roast: Marvin is an Israeli startup using satellite imagery, climate models, and AI to help agricultural value chains figure out what is actually happening on the ground before the crop fails or the supply chain snaps. They are not selling dashboards to sustainability teams. They are selling operational intelligence to buyers who lose money when the forecast is wrong. Food companies trying to lock long-term supply in a world where reliability is collapsing. Insurers repricing parametric ag coverage because loss ratios just broke their models. Governments trying to manage subsidy programs without field inspectors. You have spent two decades explaining to skeptical customers why paying for earth observation beats guessing. You sold Shell on proactive monitoring when their default was reactive cleanup. You sold GFW funders on why satellite AIS was worth the price when broadcast AIS was free. The Marvin pitch is structurally identical. The buyer has Sentinel data they do not use, agronomists who cannot scale, and supply risk they cannot quantify. What they need is someone who understands both the technical stack and the commercial argument well enough to make the pilot feel inevitable. The company is small, probably ten to twenty people, venture-backed, still defining the revenue model. A partnerships or BD role there is not joining a machine. It is building the machine. You like that part. The problem is always the same. Can a climate data company that should sell to everyone focus long enough to dominate one vertical before the funding runs out. If you join, your job is to answer that question faster than they burn cash.

watch_list.why: Just closed $350M credit facility, SAR + maritime domain awareness, overlaps with Paul's KSAT and GFW background
watch_list.roast: ICEYE is a Finnish SAR satellite company that just locked a $350 million credit facility from a seven-bank syndicate, which tells you two things. One, they have revenue. Two, the revenue is probably coming from defense and intelligence customers who pay on time and do not negotiate. They have been flying SAR smallsats since 2018, building a constellation for persistent monitoring, and selling radar imagery and intelligence products to governments, insurers, and maritime customers. This is your lane. You spent a decade at KSAT explaining to oil majors why radar does not care about clouds and to government buyers why revisit matters more than resolution. You know what it takes to move a SAR capability from a tech demo into a service contract that renews. ICEYE has done that at scale. They have customers. They have satellites. They have a business model that works. What they probably do not have is someone who understands both the satellite operator perspective and the buyer perspective well enough to build partnerships that are not just data resale. You negotiated Planet imagery at a ninety-five percent discount by understanding what each party actually needed. You built the ASN subsea cable partnership by recognizing a non-obvious application for vessel tracking data. ICEYE needs that skill set. The company is past the startup romance phase. This is industrial execution. Deliver the tasking, close the contract, scale the revenue. If you are looking for a mission-driven save-the-world vibe, this is not it. If you are looking for a commercial role where your EO and maritime domain experience converts directly into leverage, start the conversation.

morning_briefs.notes: Daily / Morning Brief cron commit landed at 12:30 UTC but no morning_briefs entry was appended to dashboard-data.json (existing pipeline bug, last appended entry was 2026-05-01). This run filled the gap. Digest had 276 postings of which ~225 were aggregator-board URLs (Terra.do, Climatebase, Climate Change Jobs by city, Schmidt Marine listings, Esri portfolio, etc.) that link to listing pages rather than specific JDs and are unscoreable per rubric. After applying applications-dedup, watch_list checks, hard exclusions, title-level filtering for engineering/analyst/IC-SDR/recruiter roles, and the recently-ranked window, 8 specific JDs were dispatched to scoring subagents. Subagents could not fetch JDs from their sandboxed environment (WebFetch denied), so fetching was retried in main context. Sierra Club returned 403 (unreadable). 5 successful fetches plus 2 hard-exclusion title-checks plus 1 already-known-pattern (Bentley) produced 4 new Tier 3 dashboard entries: Bees & Bears Head of Risk Berlin (44.5), Bentley Principal Consultant Remote/GA (47.5), SF Estuary Institute Executive Director Richmond CA (52.0), Active Site Chief of Staff Cambridge MA (43.5). No Tier 1 or Tier 2 entries today, no deep dives triggered.
